items.question: You deploy k-NN on customer data with income (0–500k) and age (0–100) without scaling. The interviewer asks why performance is poor—what do you say?
items.answer: Income dominates Euclidean distance because squared differences on income are orders of magnitude larger than on age. I would put StandardScaler in a pipeline before KNeighborsClassifier, then cross-validate k and Minkowski p. I would also spot-check `kneighbors` on a few rows to confirm neighbors differ meaningfully across age and income.

items.question: Explain the relationship between Minkowski distance, Manhattan distance and Euclidean distance.
items.answer: Minkowski distance is d_p(u,v) = (Σ|uᵢ−vᵢ|^p)^(1/p). p=1 gives Manhattan (sum of absolute gaps); p=2 gives Euclidean (square root of sum of squared gaps). Libraries like scikit-learn expose this as `metric='minkowski'` with parameter p.

items.question: When would you choose cosine similarity over Euclidean distance for a RAG vector store?
items.answer: When embedding magnitude correlates with document length or batch artifacts rather than semantics, cosine compares direction and ignores scale. I would normalize or use cosine indexing, then evaluate recall@k on labeled query–document pairs. If magnitude encodes useful signal even after normalization, I would compare both metrics on the same benchmark.

items.question: Can you use Manhattan distance with standard k-means? Why or why not?
items.answer: Standard k-means minimizes within-cluster sum of squared Euclidean distances and updates centroids as coordinate means—that derivation assumes L2 geometry. For L1-style clustering you need a different method such as k-medoids; sklearn KMeans does not accept a Manhattan metric parameter.
